label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out the answer to this question. The story is about an employer who is settling accounts with his children's governess, Julia Vassilyevna. The employer starts by saying they agreed on thirty rubles a month, but Julia had said forty. He then proceeds to deduct various amounts, like for Sundays, holidays, days the kids were sick, her having a toothache, breaking a teacup, the kids getting into accidents, and so on. Each time he subtracts some rubles. At the end, he gives her eleven rubles and then gets angry when she thanks him. He later gives her the full eighty rubles, saying it was a cruel lesson. The question is asking why he made those criticisms about her work. Looking at the choices: A) She had neglected her duties. B) He wanted to pay her as little money as possible. C) He wanted to make her realize that she was being cheated and protest against it. D) He wanted to make her feel miserable. Now, let's think. The employer is bringing up all these reasons to deduct money. But at the end, he realizes that she didn't protest and was just being passive. He says he's giving her the full eighty rubles as a lesson and mentions how easy it is to crush the weak. The key here is the employer's intention. He wasn't actually trying to pay her less because she was neglecting duties (maybe she wasn't, as he later says she's weak and spineless). The employer seems to be testing her by trying to give her less money, maybe to see if she would stand up for herself. He gets angry that she just accepts it without protesting, and then gives her the full amount as a lesson. So his intention was to make her realize she was being cheated and to protest against it. Therefore, the answer is C. But let me check again. When he gives her the eleven rubles, he gets angry and says, "Why this 'merci'?" and she says
